Q1: Honestly, my study routine was chaotic at best. I'm juggling so much with limited resources that I couldn't stick to a consistent schedule. I'd often study in bursts—like downloading everything I could when I had wifi access, then working through materials offline whenever I found pockets of time. I tried to dedicate about 15 hours a week, but those hours were scattered across late nights and early mornings between other responsibilities. It was exhausting, but I made it work somehow.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on deadlines and what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything because I was dealing with work or family stuff, then I'd have these intense cramming sessions right before something was due. I wished I could be more regular, but consistency felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. The anxiety of falling behind was constant, even when I was technically keeping up with assignments.
Q3: Usually panic prompted me to log in—realizing a deadline was approaching or seeing a notification about something new posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important because my attendance was so low and I was terrified of falling behind. The fear of missing a crucial update or assignment kept me checking more than any genuine excitement about the content, if I'm being honest.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never felt like a natural part of my routine—it was more like a source of stress I had to face. I'd sometimes get into a rhythm for a week or two, but then life would interrupt and I'd fall off again. The closest it got to routine was my habit of downloading everything at once so I could study offline, but even that was driven by necessity rather than comfort with the platform.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful because I learn best by doing and seeing things demonstrated. I'd watch them, pause constantly, and try to work through examples with my hands—writing, drawing diagrams, whatever helped it stick. Discussion posts were useful too because seeing how other students approached problems gave me different perspectives. The downloadable resources were lifesavers since I could print them and study anywhere.
Q6: I mostly ignored the optional readings and supplementary materials because I simply didn't have the bandwidth. When you're already stretched thin and anxious about completing required work, optional stuff feels like a trap that'll just make you more behind. I also didn't engage much with live sessions because my schedule was unpredictable and I couldn't always be online at specific times. It wasn't that I didn't value them—I just had to prioritize survival.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or exams. I know that sounds mercenary, but when you're overwhelmed, you have to be strategic about where you put your energy. If I could see a clear line between 'doing this thing' and 'getting a better grade,' I'd do it. Anything that felt abstract or loosely connected to outcomes got pushed aside, even if it might have been enriching.
Q8: Optional activities felt like traps, honestly. They'd sit there making me feel guilty for not doing them, adding to my anxiety without adding to my grade. Required activities at least had clear stakes—I knew exactly what would happen if I didn't complete them. With optional stuff, I'd constantly wonder if I was missing something crucial by skipping them, which just added to my stress without providing any real motivation to engage.
Q9: Oh, absolutely. Most of my actual studying happened offline with downloaded materials, handwritten notes, and sometimes textbooks I borrowed. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so I need to physically write things out, create flashcards, and work through problems on paper. The LMS was really just a delivery system for me—I'd grab what I needed and then do the real learning away from the screen, often without internet access.
Q10: There were so many times I was studying intensively but the LMS would show nothing. I'd spend hours working through printed lecture notes at the library, or I'd be practicing problems in a notebook while riding the bus. Sometimes I'd watch a video once, download the slides, and then spend days working through them offline. My actual engagement was way higher than my login frequency would suggest.
Q11: Yes, constantly. That was basically my whole strategy. I'd access materials once, download everything I could, and then work with them offline for days or even weeks. With my limited internet access and unpredictable schedule, it was the only way I could make it work. I'd return to the same downloaded PDFs and videos multiple times, but the system would only show that initial access.
Q12: There were definitely periods where everything felt impossible. Around midterms, my anxiety spiked so badly that I could barely focus on anything. I'd sit there staring at materials without absorbing anything. Family obligations would pile up, or I'd be exhausted from working, and the course would feel like this insurmountable mountain. The CBT sessions helped me recognize these patterns, but in the moment, it was really hard to push through.
Q13: Absolutely, all of those things affected me constantly. When I was exhausted from everything else in my life, I'd log in but just kind of zombie-scroll through the LMS without really engaging. Confusion was a big one too—sometimes I'd avoid logging in because I didn't understand something and facing that confusion felt overwhelming. Competing responsibilities meant I was always triaging, and the LMS often lost to more immediate demands.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about money or family stuff. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions so I could feel like I'd 'done something.' Those sessions felt hollow—I was present in the system but completely absent mentally. My anxiety made it hard to actually absorb anything.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because they required mental energy I didn't always have. Writing something coherent when you're anxious and exhausted is really hard, even when you know the material. I'd also avoid starting new modules because beginning something new felt more daunting than continuing something familiar. The avoidance made the anxiety worse, which made me avoid more—it was a vicious cycle I struggled with all semester.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage. I'd have periods of almost no activity, then intense bursts right before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but it was how I survived. The deadline pressure actually helped cut through my anxiety sometimes—when there was no choice but to act, I could finally focus. But it also meant I was constantly in crisis mode, which was exhausting and probably affected my assignment scores.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. Good feedback would give me a temporary boost, but bad feedback could send me spiraling for days. I started avoiding checking my grades because the anxiety of not knowing felt better than the potential devastation of a bad score. When I did get constructive feedback, I tried to use it, but my emotional reaction often got in the way of actually learning from it.
Q18: The course structure with 20 online components was honestly overwhelming. Having so many modules made it hard to see the big picture or feel any sense of progress. I appreciated that things were broken into smaller pieces, but it also meant constant deadlines and constant anxiety about what I might be missing. The flexibility was good for my unpredictable schedule, but the sheer volume was intimidating.
Q19: The ability to download materials made engagement possible for me—without that, I couldn't have succeeded. But the platform could be confusing to navigate, and sometimes I'd waste precious time just trying to find things. The discussion requirements felt forced sometimes, like engagement for engagement's sake rather than genuine learning. Clearer organization and fewer but more meaningful activities would have helped me manage my anxiety better.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a bad week—logging in repeatedly out of anxiety, clicking around without actually submitting anything because nothing feels good enough. Or maybe they're trying to understand something but struggling, so they keep accessing materials without being able to complete the work. High activity doesn't mean productive activity, especially when you're overwhelmed or confused.
Q21: That's literally me. My LMS activity was pretty low, but I got a distinction because I was doing most of my real learning offline. I'd download things once and study them extensively away from the platform. Someone looking at my login frequency would think I was barely engaged, but I was actually putting in serious work—just not in ways the system could track. The LMS only sees a fraction of real engagement.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when people are active, what they click on, whether they meet deadlines. But they completely miss the quality of engagement and all the learning that happens offline. They can't tell if someone is anxiously clicking around without absorbing anything versus deeply engaged with downloaded materials. For someone like me with limited resources who studies mostly offline, the logs would dramatically underestimate my actual effort.
Q23: Being engaged means actually wrestling with the material—thinking about it, questioning it, connecting it to other things I know. It's not about time spent logged in or videos watched. For me, engagement often looks like sitting with printed notes, working through problems by hand, or explaining concepts to myself out loud. It's an internal process that doesn't always have external markers, especially digital ones.
Q24: The LMS would never show how hard I fought my anxiety to complete this course, or the hours I spent studying on printed materials away from any screen. It wouldn't capture the mental energy required to push through when everything felt impossible, or the genuine learning that happened during my offline study sessions. It also wouldn't show how surprised I am that I actually succeeded—I kept expecting to fail despite doing everything I could.
